text: A sleeve gastrectomy is the most common procedure done in the United States today for weight loss. It consists of removing a large portion of the stomach, which will lead to a seventy percent reduction in excess body weight, as well as an improvement or resolution in such medical problems as high blood pressure, sleep apnea, diabetes. This is an example of a robotic sleeve gastrectomy.

The robotic instruments bend like fingers as the surgeon uses a vessel sealer to disconnect the blood supply from the portion of the stomach to be removed. The sealer simultaneously seals and cuts the vessels, ensuring precision without bleeding. The surgeon works towards the diaphragm and spleen, visible as a reddish structure on the right, releasing the upper portion of the stomach to allow the esophagus to join through the diaphragm.

The surgeon completes the dissection along the greater curvature of the stomach using a robotic instrument, which bends without causing bleeding. The area is inspected for any adhesions or connections to the pancreas, confirming none are present. A tube is placed into the stomach to position it correctly, followed by the introduction of a smart stapler from the da Vinci robot to measure tissue thickness and ensure appropriate staple length for transection.

The surgeon uses a robotic stapler to conform to the patient's anatomy, snugly positioning it against the tube to remove the majority of the stomach. The sleeve is visible on the left side, while the portion of the stomach to be removed is on the right. The stapler fires once more to complete the sleeve gastrectomy.

The surgeon sets aside the resected portion of the stomach and prepares for a leak test by irrigating the staple line and applying air pressure. No bubbling or leaking is observed. The surgeon removes excess fluid and the tube, then places the resected stomach in a bag for extraction through the abdominal wall.
This is Dr. Thomas Brown for Colorado Bariatric Surgery Institute. You can find us on coloradobariatric.com.
